content.text: Every token an LLM emits is the result of one decision: given the probability distribution over the vocabulary, which token gets appended to the sequence?
Decoding methods

are the algorithms that make that decision. Choosing the right strategy—greedy argmax, beam search, temperature-scaled sampling, nucleus sampling, or top-k sampling—determines the reliability, diversity, latency, and cost of everything an agent produces.
What it is
After a forward pass through the transformer, the model produces a logit vector
over its entire vocabulary
. Applying softmax (optionally scaled by temperature
) converts those logits into a probability distribution:

A decoding method is any algorithm that selects the next token from this distribution and appends it to the sequence. The process repeats—prefixing the new token as additional context—until an end-of-sequence token is produced or a maximum length is reached. Because each step conditions on all preceding tokens, the choice made at step
permanently affects every subsequent step.
Three broad families exist.
Greedy decoding
picks
at every step—fast and deterministic but myopic.
Beam search
maintains
partial hypotheses simultaneously, expanding and pruning by cumulative log-probability, trading compute for a better global sequence score.
Stochastic sampling
draws from the distribution (possibly filtered by temperature, top-p, or top-k) introducing randomness that yields diversity and avoids repetition loops at the cost of output variance.
Why it matters

For agentic AI systems the decoding method is not an academic detail—it is an operational decision with direct consequences for reliability and cost. Agent loops produce structured outputs such as JSON tool calls, Python code snippets, or chain-of-thought reasoning traces. A badly chosen decoding strategy can make those outputs unparseable, loop indefinitely on repetitive tokens, or require multiple expensive retries.

The stakes are amplified by the autoregressive nature of generation: an agent cannot revise a token once it has been emitted. Greedy decoding at temperature 0 maximizes per-step confidence and structural predictability for tool-call JSON. Beam search can improve quality of short constrained outputs such as SQL or regular expressions but fails to integrate with tool-call interrupts that break the generation boundary mid-sequence. Stochastic sampling enables creative and open-ended outputs needed for user-facing prose but introduces variance that must be managed by validation, retry logic, and appropriate temperature tuning.
Practically, every AI inference API exposes parameters such as
temperature
top_p
top_k
, and
num_beams
precisely because no single setting is correct across all tasks. Understanding what each parameter does at the probability-distribution level is the prerequisite for tuning agents reliably.
Benefits
Greedy decoding
— minimal latency (one argmax per step), fully deterministic with fixed model weights, zero additional memory overhead, trivially streamable token by token
Beam search

— explores multiple hypotheses before committing, can produce globally better-scoring sequences than greedy for short outputs, effective for constrained generation tasks like translation and captioning
Temperature sampling
— produces diverse and creative outputs, breaks repetition loops that greedy falls into, allows smooth interpolation between deterministic (T→0) and random (T→∞) generation
Top-p (nucleus) sampling
— adapts the candidate pool size to the confidence of the distribution, reducing the risk of drawing incoherent low-probability tokens without collapsing diversity
Top-k sampling
— hard upper bound on candidate pool size, predictable behavior, simple to reason about and implement
Deterministic modes
— enable regression testing with fixed seeds, reproducible evals, and easier debugging of agent failures
Trade-offs
Greedy

: myopic—a locally optimal token can lead the sequence into a dead end with no mechanism to backtrack. Prone to repetition loops when the prefix makes repeated tokens consistently highest probability.
Greedy
: temperature 0 collapses the distribution; the model cannot express uncertainty or offer alternative continuations, which matters when downstream validation could benefit from a confidence signal.
Beam search
: compute cost scales as
per step rather than
; KV cache must be maintained for all b beams simultaneously, multiplying memory.
Beam search
: incompatible with the agent pattern of interrupting generation mid-stream at a tool-call delimiter and resuming with tool results; beam hypotheses become incoherent across an external state change.
Beam search
: length-normalization heuristics are required to avoid preferring short outputs, and choosing the right normalization factor is task-specific.
Temperature sampling
: at high temperatures (T > 1) the distribution approaches uniform and outputs lose coherence; at very low temperatures the method converges toward greedy with repetition.
Top-p / top-k
: hyperparameters that must be tuned per task and model; wrong values can eliminate diversity or admit too many low-quality tokens into the candidate set.
All sampling methods
: non-deterministic by default; reproducibility requires a fixed random seed, which may not be exposed by all hosted inference APIs.
How it works
Greedy decoding
At each step the model performs a forward pass and returns the last-position logit vector. Greedy decoding applies softmax and picks the argmax:

Temperature does not alter the argmax for positive T (the index of the maximum is scale-invariant), but setting T→0 before sampling converges to greedy behavior on APIs that expose only sampling interfaces. The full output sequence
is produced left-to-right with no lookahead and no branching.
Beam search
Beam search maintains a set of
partial sequences called the beam. At each step every sequence in the beam is expanded by its top-
candidate next tokens, yielding up to
candidates. These are scored by cumulative log-probability with optional length normalization and the best
are retained:
The length penalty exponent

(typically 0.6–0.8) prevents the search from preferring shorter sequences that accumulate fewer negative log-probabilities. When a beam emits EOS it moves to the completed set; search continues until all beams complete or a maximum length is reached.
Temperature scaling
Temperature
reshapes the distribution before sampling by dividing logits:

When T < 1 the distribution sharpens, concentrating probability mass on the most likely tokens. When T > 1 the distribution flattens, spreading mass across more vocabulary entries. T = 1 leaves the model's learned distribution unchanged.
Top-k sampling
Top-k sampling restricts the candidate pool to the k highest-probability tokens, re-normalizes over that set, and samples:
where
. The fixed k is a weakness: when the model is confident, k=50 still admits many near-zero probability tokens; when uncertain, k=5 may eliminate valid alternatives.
Top-p (nucleus) sampling

Top-p sampling (Holtzman et al., 2020) adapts the candidate pool size to the distribution's sharpness. Tokens are sorted in descending probability order; the smallest set whose cumulative probability meets or exceeds p is selected, and sampling occurs within that nucleus:

attrs.latex: V_p = \underset{V' \subseteq V}{\arg\min} |V'| \text{ s.t. } \sum_{w \in V'} P(x_t=w) \geq p

content.text: When the model is confident,

may contain only 2–5 tokens; when uncertain, it may span hundreds. This adaptive behavior makes top-p more robust than top-k across varying model confidence. Values p = 0.9 to 0.95 are common defaults for open-ended generation.
Architecture
The diagram below shows how logits flow through the shared softmax pipeline and then branch into the three decoding strategies, each with its own selection rule and use-case profile.

The shared prefix (model forward pass → logits → softmax/temperature) is identical regardless of decoding strategy. The branching happens at the selection step. Greedy and sampling operate on a single forward pass per token; beam search runs the same forward pass
times (or a batched equivalent) to score all live hypotheses before pruning.
When to use it
Greedy / temperature 0
Agent tool calls that produce JSON, XML, or other parseable structured formats
Code generation where correctness is binary and reproducibility is required
Chain-of-thought reasoning when you want deterministic audit trails
Any step where a validation gate or retry loop catches errors
Regression testing: fixed seed + greedy makes agent behavior deterministic across runs
Beam search
Offline batch tasks: document summarization, machine translation, image captioning
Short constrained outputs (< 50 tokens) where global coherence matters more than latency
Do NOT use beam search when generation must pause mid-sequence to execute a tool call
Do NOT use beam search when output length is unbounded (long chain-of-thought, stories)
Stochastic sampling
Open-ended creative writing, story generation, or brainstorming
User-facing conversational replies where diverse phrasing improves perceived quality
Best-of-N generation: sample N completions at moderate temperature, pick the best
Exploration during agent development when greedy produces repetitive outputs
Do NOT use high temperatures for structured outputs—parse errors will dominate
How to implement
The following steps describe implementing each decoding method from scratch in Python, which is the clearest way to understand the mechanics before relying on an inference framework.
Obtain the final-position logit vector from the model (shape: [vocab_size]).
Apply temperature scaling by dividing logits by T before softmax.
For greedy: call argmax on the logits.
For top-k: mask all but the k largest logits to -inf, apply softmax, and multinomial sample.
For top-p: sort by probability descending, compute cumulative sum, mask tokens beyond the nucleus, resample.
For beam search: maintain a list of (token_ids, log_score) tuples; expand each beam, score, prune to width b.
Append the selected token id, re-run the model with the extended context, repeat until EOS or max_tokens.

import numpy as np
from typing import List

# Utility: softmax with optional temperature
def softmax(logits: np.ndarray, temperature: float = 1.0) -> np.ndarray:
    logits = logits / max(temperature, 1e-8)
    e = np.exp(logits - logits.max())  # numerical stability
    return e / e.sum()

# ---- Greedy decoding ----
def greedy_step(logits: np.ndarray) -> int:
    """Select the highest-probability token."""
    return int(np.argmax(logits))

# ---- Top-k sampling ----
def top_k_step(logits: np.ndarray, k: int = 50, temperature: float = 1.0) -> int:
    """Sample from the top-k highest-logit tokens."""
    top_k_indices = np.argpartition(logits, -k)[-k:]
    top_k_logits = logits[top_k_indices]
    probs = softmax(top_k_logits, temperature)
    chosen = np.random.choice(len(probs), p=probs)
    return int(top_k_indices[chosen])

# ---- Top-p (nucleus) sampling ----
def top_p_step(logits: np.ndarray, p: float = 0.9, temperature: float = 1.0) -> int:
    """Sample from the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative prob >= p."""
    probs = softmax(logits, temperature)
    sorted_idx = np.argsort(probs)[::-1]           # descending order
    sorted_probs = probs[sorted_idx]
    cumulative = np.cumsum(sorted_probs)
    # keep tokens until cumulative prob reaches p
    nucleus_mask = cumulative - sorted_probs < p
    nucleus_probs = sorted_probs * nucleus_mask
    nucleus_probs /= nucleus_probs.sum()
    chosen_rank = np.random.choice(len(nucleus_probs), p=nucleus_probs)
    return int(sorted_idx[chosen_rank])

# ---- Full greedy decode loop (toy model stub) ----
def greedy_decode(model, prompt_ids: List[int], max_tokens: int = 256, eos_id: int = 2) -> List[int]:
    tokens = list(prompt_ids)
    for _ in range(max_tokens):
        logits = model(tokens)  # returns np.ndarray of shape [vocab_size]
        next_id = greedy_step(logits)
        tokens.append(next_id)
        if next_id == eos_id:
            break
    return tokens
Examples
Beam search implementation

import math
from typing import List, Tuple

def beam_search(
    model,                   # callable: ids -> np.ndarray logits (last position)
    prompt_ids: List[int],
    beam_width: int = 4,
    max_new_tokens: int = 64,
    length_penalty: float = 0.7,
    eos_id: int = 2,
) -> List[int]:
    """
    Beam search returning the highest-scoring completed sequence.
    Simplified (non-batched) for clarity.
    """
    import numpy as np

    def log_softmax(logits):
        shifted = logits - logits.max()
        return shifted - np.log(np.sum(np.exp(shifted)))

    # Each beam: (token_ids, log_score)
    beams: List[Tuple[List[int], float]] = [(list(prompt_ids), 0.0)]
    completed = []

    for _ in range(max_new_tokens):
        if not beams:
            break
        candidates = []
        for ids, score in beams:
            log_probs = log_softmax(model(ids))
            # expand with top beam_width candidates
            top_ids = np.argpartition(log_probs, -beam_width)[-beam_width:]
            for tok in top_ids:
                candidates.append((ids + [int(tok)], score + float(log_probs[tok])))

        def normalized(c):
            ids, sc = c
            n = max(len(ids) - len(prompt_ids), 1)
            return sc / n ** length_penalty

        candidates.sort(key=normalized, reverse=True)
        beams = []
        for ids, sc in candidates[:beam_width * 2]:
            if ids[-1] == eos_id:
                n = max(len(ids) - len(prompt_ids), 1)
                completed.append((ids, sc / n ** length_penalty))
            else:
                beams.append((ids, sc))
            if len(beams) == beam_width:
                break

    if not completed:
        completed = [(ids, sc / max(len(ids)-len(prompt_ids),1)**length_penalty)
                     for ids, sc in beams]
    return max(completed, key=lambda x: x[1])[0]
Comparing strategies with an OpenAI-compatible API

import os
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(api_key=os.environ["OPENAI_API_KEY"])

PROMPT = "Summarize the transformer architecture in exactly three bullet points."

def call(temperature: float, top_p: float, label: str) -> str:
    resp = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": PROMPT}],
        temperature=temperature,
        top_p=top_p,
        max_tokens=200,
        seed=42,          # for reproducibility in near-greedy calls
    )
    tokens = resp.usage.completion_tokens
    text = resp.choices[0].message.content
    print(f"[{label:12s}] tokens={tokens:3d}  text[:80]={text[:80]!r}")
    return text

# temperature=0 collapses to greedy-equivalent on most APIs
greedy_out = call(0,   1.0, "greedy")
low_t_out  = call(0.2, 0.9, "low-temp")
sample_out = call(0.9, 0.95,"creative")

# Best-of-3: sample three times and pick the one with most bullet points
candidates = [call(0.8, 0.9, f"sample-{i}") for i in range(3)]
best = max(candidates, key=lambda t: t.count("-") + t.count("•"))
print("\nBest-of-3 winner:\n", best)
Real-world example

Consider a production agentic pipeline that answers customer support questions by searching a knowledge base and writing a structured response. The pipeline has two sequential generation steps with different decoding requirements.
Step 1 — Tool call generation

: The agent must emit a JSON object specifying a search query. This step uses temperature = 0. Any deviation from valid JSON causes a parse error that aborts the turn. Greedy minimizes parse failures and keeps the step reproducible for debugging.
Step 2 — Answer synthesis

: The agent writes a customer-facing prose response in 2–4 sentences. This step uses temperature = 0.7, top_p = 0.9. Diverse phrasing improves user experience. The structure is free-form so there is nothing to parse.

import json, os
from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI(api_key=os.environ["OPENAI_API_KEY"])

def run_agent_turn(user_query: str) -> str:
    # ---- Step 1: structured tool call — greedy ----
    tool_resp = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content":
                'Output ONLY valid JSON: {"query": "<search terms>"}'},
            {"role": "user", "content": user_query},
        ],
        temperature=0,               # greedy: reproducible, structurally reliable
        max_tokens=64,
        response_format={"type": "json_object"},
    )
    payload = json.loads(tool_resp.choices[0].message.content)
    results = knowledge_base_search(payload["query"])

    # ---- Step 2: prose synthesis — sampling ----
    synthesis_resp = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful customer support agent."},
            {"role": "user",      "content": user_query},
            {"role": "assistant", "content": f"[search]: {results}"},
            {"role": "user",      "content": "Write a concise, friendly answer."},
        ],
        temperature=0.7,             # sampling: natural, varied phrasing
        top_p=0.9,
        max_tokens=200,
    )
    return synthesis_resp.choices[0].message.content

def knowledge_base_search(query: str) -> str:
    # Stub — replace with real vector search
    return f"Result for '{query}': orders ship within 3-5 business days."

if __name__ == "__main__":
    print(run_agent_turn("When will my order arrive?"))

This two-temperature pattern—greedy for structure, sampling for prose—is standard practice in production agent systems. It separates reliability concerns (JSON validity) from quality concerns (phrasing diversity) so each failure mode can be addressed independently.
Common mistakes
Expecting beam search to fix hallucinations

: Beam search optimizes cumulative log-probability, not factual accuracy. A confident but wrong completion will outscore a correct but less probable one. Hallucinations are a training-distribution problem, not a decoding problem.
Using high temperature for tool calls

: Temperature = 1.0 on a JSON tool-call step dramatically increases the rate of malformed JSON. Even T = 0.5 can introduce unexpected token variations that break parsing. Always use T = 0 or T ≤ 0.1 for structured steps.
Wide beams on long outputs

: Setting beam_width = 10 on a 500-token chain-of-thought multiplies memory and compute 10× while providing negligible quality improvement over greedy. Beam search is only useful when the globally optimal short sequence differs materially from the greedy path.
Assuming temperature = 0 is identical across implementations
: Some inference engines sample at T = 0 due to numerical precision (multiple tokens can share the maximum logit after fp16 rounding). Verify determinism empirically with the target model and API.
Ignoring top_p when combining with high temperature
: Setting temperature = 1.5 with top_p = 1.0 admits extremely low-probability tokens that can catastrophically derail generation. Always pair high temperatures with top_p ≤ 0.95.
Treating greedy output as ground truth
: Greedy is locally optimal per step but not globally optimal. For high-stakes generation, complement greedy with logprob inspection or best-of-N sampling to detect low-confidence decisions.
Hard-coding decoding params across all agent steps
: A single set of parameters rarely works optimally for all stages of an agent loop. Audit each generation call and set params appropriate to its output type and failure mode.
Best practices
Set temperature = 0 for every agent step that outputs parseable structures: JSON, XML, Python, SQL, or regex
Reserve sampling (temperature 0.7–0.9, top_p 0.9–0.95) for natural-language output steps where diversity improves quality
Prefer best-of-N sampling over wide beam search when exploring alternatives: generate N responses with moderate temperature, score them with a validator or reward model, pick the best
Use the API's native structured-output or JSON-mode features in preference to decoding hacks; they apply grammar constraints that are more reliable than temperature tuning alone
Log the decoding parameters used for every generation call in agent traces to enable reproducible debugging and eval
When a greedy agent loops or stalls, increase temperature slightly (0.1–0.3) or add a repetition_penalty rather than switching to beam search
Validate all structured outputs with a schema parser before using them downstream; catch decoding failures early in the loop
Run evals with both fixed-seed greedy and sampled decoding to understand output variance before deploying to production
Performance and scalability
Latency analysis

All decoding strategies share the same per-step cost for a single beam: one transformer forward pass over the full context. The time-to-last-token scales as O(n) forward passes for an n-token output. The KV cache makes each forward pass O(n · d²) where d is the model dimension, so latency grows super-linearly with context length during the decode phase.

Beam search multiplies the per-step compute by b. For beam_width = 4 and output length 100, beam search costs roughly 4× the GPU compute of greedy for the same output length. This is rarely justified for interactive agents with latency budgets under 5 seconds.
Memory
Greedy and sampling need a KV cache for one sequence. Beam search needs b KV caches simultaneously until all beams complete. On a 7B-parameter model with bf16 KV cache and context length 4096:

attrs.latex: \text{KV size} \approx 2 \times L \times H \times d_h \times S \times 2 \text{ bytes}

content.text: For Llama-3-8B (32 layers, 32 heads, 128 head_dim, 4096 context) this is roughly 1 GB per sequence. Beam width 4 multiplies this to 4 GB for KV alone, before model weights. This makes wide beam search prohibitive in multi-user serving environments.
Throughput

Temperature, top-p, and top-k add negligible overhead over greedy because they operate on the last-position logits after the forward pass. The sort in top-p is O(V log V), but V ≈ 32 000–128 000 is trivially fast on a GPU. The primary throughput lever is batching multiple requests together; decoding strategy choice is a secondary concern.
Tooling and ecosystem
Inference frameworks
Hugging Face Transformers — GenerationConfig
: Unified generate() API covering greedy, beam_search, top_k, top_p, temperature, diverse beam search. Use do_sample=False for greedy/beam, do_sample=True for sampling.
vLLM
: High-throughput PagedAttention-based serving; exposes SamplingParams(temperature, top_p, top_k). Beam search is intentionally not supported—vLLM targets throughput with greedy/sampling.
llama.cpp / Ollama

: CPU and GPU inference; supports temperature, top_p, top_k, repeat_penalty. The repeat_penalty applies a multiplicative discount to logits of recently seen tokens, mitigating greedy repetition loops.
TensorRT-LLM
: NVIDIA optimized engine; supports beam search for encoder-decoder models and sampling for decoder-only. Beam search runs as a batched operation for GPU efficiency.
Outlines / Guidance / LMQL
: Grammar-constrained decoding libraries that mask invalid tokens at each step, guaranteeing schema-valid output regardless of decoding strategy.

# Hugging Face GenerationConfig examples
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer, GenerationConfig

model_id = "meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B"
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_id)
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)
inputs = tokenizer("The Eiffel Tower is located in", return_tensors="pt")

# Greedy
greedy_cfg = GenerationConfig(max_new_tokens=32, do_sample=False)

# Nucleus sampling
nucleus_cfg = GenerationConfig(
    max_new_tokens=64, do_sample=True,
    temperature=0.8, top_p=0.92, top_k=0,
)

# Beam search
beam_cfg = GenerationConfig(
    max_new_tokens=32, num_beams=5,
    length_penalty=0.8, early_stopping=True, do_sample=False,
)

for cfg, label in [(greedy_cfg, "greedy"), (nucleus_cfg, "nucleus"), (beam_cfg, "beam")]:
    out = model.generate(**inputs, generation_config=cfg)
    print(f"[{label}]", tokenizer.decode(out[0][inputs['input_ids'].shape[1]:],
                                          skip_special_tokens=True))

Grammar-constrained decoding (Outlines, LMQL, Guidance) is increasingly preferred for structured agent outputs in production because it provides a guarantee greedy sampling cannot: the generated text is guaranteed to conform to a grammar, not merely likely to. Prefer constrained decoding over temperature tuning when the target inference stack supports it.
Security and reliability
Prompt injection via high-variance sampling

High-temperature sampling increases the probability that a carefully crafted adversarial prefix in retrieved context can steer generation toward harmful or incorrect completions. An attacker embedding a malicious suffix in a tool result can exploit the widened candidate pool. Mitigation: use low temperature (≤ 0.3) for any generation step that processes untrusted content; apply output validation regardless of temperature setting.
Repetition and degenerate outputs

Greedy decoding is vulnerable to repetition loops where a prefix makes the most likely next token identical to the preceding tokens, creating infinite repetition until max_tokens is reached. This is a reliability failure that wastes the token budget without producing useful output. Mitigations: set repetition_penalty > 1.0 (many frameworks), apply frequency_penalty in OpenAI-compatible APIs, or use a low positive temperature as a fallback when repetition is detected.
Reproducibility for audit

Production agents making consequential decisions (financial, medical, legal) must be reproducible for audit purposes. Use greedy decoding (temperature = 0) and log the model version, context hash, and decoding parameters for every generation call. If sampling is required, record and replay the random seed. Most hosted APIs do not guarantee seed reproducibility across model version updates—validate this assumption before relying on it in audit-sensitive systems.
Validate all structured outputs with a schema parser before acting on them, regardless of decoding strategy
Set max_tokens conservatively to cap exposure from repetition loops
Monitor token-per-second rates; sudden drops can indicate repetition loops consuming the token budget
Use logit_bias or post-processing to block known unsafe token sequences in safety-critical deployments
Prefer constrained decoding APIs over temperature tuning for guaranteed structural safety
Mental model
Think of decoding as navigating a tree of possible continuations. Each node is a token, each edge is a probability, and the model assigns weights to all edges leaving a node simultaneously.
Greedy decoding

always takes the single heaviest edge—like a hiker who always chooses the most-trodden path at every fork. The hiker moves fast and predictably but may descend into a dead-end valley because the locally optimal step led away from the globally best route.
Beam search

is a team of b hikers who split at every fork, each taking a different promising path. After a fixed number of steps the team compares progress (cumulative log-probability) and the weakest hikers are eliminated. The team eventually converges on the globally best short path—but b× slower than a single hiker and unable to cross a chasm (tool call) because the team cannot re-synchronize after the detour.
Sampling

is a single hiker who rolls a weighted die at each fork. The die is biased toward the best path (temperature < 1) or toward randomness (temperature > 1). Top-p means only forks with paved roads are considered; top-k means only the k widest paths appear on the map. The hiker sometimes stumbles onto a surprisingly scenic route—or walks off a cliff.

The practical takeaway: agents are hikers crossing a landscape with structured checkpoints (tool calls, JSON delimiters). At checkpoints, use greedy—there is only one correct bridge to cross. On open terrain between checkpoints (prose synthesis, reasoning), modest sampling produces better and more natural text than relentless greedy marching.
Key takeaways
Decoding method = token selection rule
: greedy (argmax), beam (maintain b hypotheses), sampling (draw from filtered distribution). All operate on the same softmax output from the model.
Greedy dominates agentic tool calls
: temperature 0 is the correct default for any generation step producing parseable structured output. Parse errors are more costly than diversity loss.
Beam search is for offline constrained generation
: it is incompatible with mid-sequence tool interrupts, expensive in memory, and rarely justified for long outputs. Use best-of-N sampling instead when exploring multiple alternatives.
Temperature reshapes the probability distribution
: T < 1 sharpens (toward greedy), T > 1 flattens (toward random). Top-p and top-k filter the candidate pool before sampling; combine them for fine-grained control.
Top-p adapts to distribution confidence

: unlike top-k with its fixed candidate count, nucleus sampling automatically restricts to a small set when the model is confident and expands when uncertain, making it more robust across varying states.
Two-temperature pattern for agents
: temperature 0 for structural steps, temperature 0.7–0.9 for prose synthesis steps. This separates reliability from quality concerns across the agent loop.
Constrained decoding supersedes temperature tuning
: grammar-guided generation (Outlines, LMQL, Guidance) guarantees schema-valid outputs and is the preferred approach when the target inference stack supports it.
Always validate and monitor
: no decoding strategy eliminates the need for output validation, max_token limits, repetition detection, and structured-output parsing in production agents.
